nativeRange.habitat: Wolf eels inhabit rocky subtidal zones, caves, and crevices from shallow coastal water down to approximately 226 m depth. Juveniles spend up to two years drifting in open water before settling into rocky reef habitats as they mature.

diet.notes: In the wild, wolf eels have been observed consuming hard-shelled invertebrates including crabs, sea urchins, sand dollars, mussels, clams, snails, and abalone, as well as fish. Their powerful, crushing jaws are adapted for processing hard-shelled prey. In captivity, feeding typically involves whole crustaceans, bivalves, and meaty marine foods.

goodWith: large non-aggressive fish of similar size
other large cold-water Pacific species in a public exhibit context
avoidWith: other wolf eels (highly territorial toward conspecifics)
small fish that may be consumed
invertebrates (sea urchins, crabs, clams, and other hard-shelled species are natural prey)

summary: The Wolf Eel is a large, eel-like cold-water fish native to the North Pacific, capable of reaching 240 cm in length and living 20 or more years. Suitable only for large public aquarium exhibits, it is a powerful predator of hard-shelled invertebrates.
careNotes: The Wolf Eel (Anarrhichthys ocellatus) is a cold-water North Pacific species generally considered suitable only for large public aquarium exhibits or zoo facilities, and is not practical for home aquariums. Adults are reported to reach up to 240 cm in length and are known to live 20–25 years or more, making this a long-term commitment even in an institutional setting. Despite their fearsome appearance, wolf eels are generally described as curious and non-aggressive toward divers and aquarists, though they tend to be strongly territorial toward other wolf eels and will readily consume invertebrate tankmates. A tightly secured lid or tank cover is strongly advised, as wolf eels — like most eel-like fish — present a notable escape risk. Temperature must be maintained in the cold range (approximately 1–10°C); this species is not compatible with the warm water conditions of a typical tropical marine aquarium. Sexual maturity is typically reached around 7 years of age. Juveniles display a striking brick-orange coloration with dark markings that gradually fades to a gray-brown tone in adults.
breedingNotes: Wolf eels have been reported to form lifelong monogamous pair bonds. Spawning tends to occur in autumn and winter, with maturity typically not reached until approximately 7 years of age. Females produce up to 10,000 eggs per spawning event; both parents have been observed sharing parenting duties — one guarding the den while the other feeds. The egg mass has been described as grapefruit-sized, with hatching occurring after roughly 91–112 days. Captive breeding is not documented in the hobby literature and would require replicating cold North Pacific water conditions; the species is generally considered not feasible for home aquarium breeding.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as de facto primary source; the designated primary source (LiveAquaria, https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/eels) does not carry this species. FishBase provided taxonomy, size, temperature range, native range, and habitat data.

additional.name: Monterey Bay Aquarium
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (up to 2.5 m / 8.2 ft), native range (northern Baja California to Kodiak Island, west to Russia and Sea of Japan), diet (crabs, sand dollars, sea urchins, snails, abalone, mussels, clams, fish), and breeding biology (up to 10,000 eggs, 4-month incubation, lifelong pair bonds).

additional.name: Eeliad
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan (20–25 years in the wild), adult size (up to 2.4 m / 18.4 kg), native range (North Pacific from Baja California to Sea of Japan/Okhotsk), diet, monogamous pair bonding, and territorial den behavior. Cross-checked breeding biology: sexual maturity ~7 years, breeding October through winter, 91–112 day incubation.

additional.name: Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (up to 240 cm, 18.4 kg) and native range (Sea of Okhotsk and Sea of Japan to Krenitzen Islands and Imperial Beach, California). Noted that maximum lifespan is not formally established; maturity at 4–7 years implies long-lived status.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature range (1.2–10.5°C), adult size (up to 240 cm), carnivorous diet, and characterized species as 'suitable for large display tanks (public aquarium or zoo) only.'

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not documented in the hobby literature, and the cold North Pacific water conditions this species requires (approximately 1–10°C) make home aquarium breeding essentially not feasible. In the wild, pair formation occurs naturally when two animals share a den over time. Both parents guard the egg mass together for approximately 91–112 days until hatching.
